data.title: Defensive Positioning Beneath A Calm Vol Market
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities were mostly range-bound last week despite rising geopolitical risk. Equity, rates, credit, and FX volatilities all ended the week near a 1-year low (see Exhibit 1). Oil was the only notable mover, with 1M implied volatility jumping up over 8 vol pts to 36.8% (70th percentile high). As we highlighted last week, option market positioning has become increasingly bullish in oil, with outsized demand for upside calls driving an inversion in oil skew (i.e., calls are now trading at a premium to puts). This is evident in both options on the commodity (Brent/WTI) as well as USO (US Oil Fund ETF). Despite the threat of more supplies coming online from Venezuela, traders appear to be more focused on the near-term risk of potential supply disruptions stemming from the Iran unrest. Even with Trump’s escalating attacks on the Fed over the weekend, cross-asset vols appear fairly tame this morning as investors weigh the risk of Fed independence. The VIX® index is up just 1.5 pts pre-market to ~16 while FX volatility is up ~0.3 pts across the majors.

Equity Volatility: While the VIX index ended last week unch’d at 14.5%, there were notable changes underneath the index surface. S&P fixed strike vols increased modestly while investors sold calls to buy puts, leading to a meaningful steepening in put skew & convexity while the call wings came in (see Exhibit 2). These factors were enough to offset the “expected” 1.2 pt decline in the VIX index as a result of the market rally (SPX® index +1.6% wk/wk). The key takeaway from our VIX index decomposition is that defensive positioning increased meaningfully last week despite an unch’d VIX index level. The change in sentiment can also be seen in SPX 1M put skew (25D/50D ratio) which steepened to the 62nd percentile high and 1M put convexity (10D/25D ratio), which jumped from the 25th percentile low to the 72nd percentile high last week.

Correlation & Dispersion: While index vol was flat last week, single stock volatility continued to increase heading into earnings season. VIXEQSM Index gained another 2 vol pts to 36% and the spread between single stock vs. index vol widened to ~22 pts (90th percentile high; see Exhibit 3). Higher single stock vol hasn’t translated into higher index vol, however, as correlation levels have fallen to extremes. SPX 1M implied correlation, as measured by the COR1M index, fell to a near record low of 6.8% on Friday – the only time it has traded lower was a brief blip in July 2024. Stock & sector selection become more important during periods of low correlation, as the spread between winners and losers widen and potential stock-picking alpha increases.

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e., rich), while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e., cheap).

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g., lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using Z-scores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets

Cross-Asset Correlation Matrix (1M)

Cross-Asset Correlation Analysis

Macro Equity Volatility

VIX Index Volatility US International Cross-Asset

US Index Volatility

Disclaimers

There are important risks associated with transacting in any of the Cboe Company products discussed here. Before engaging in any transactions in those products, it is important for market participants to carefully review the disclosures and disclaimers contained at: Disclosures and Disclaimers Related to Cboe Options and Futures Products.

These products are complex and are suitable only for sophisticated market participants. In certain jurisdictions, Cboe Company products are only permitted for investment professionals, certified sophisticated investors, or high-net-worth corporations and associations. These products involve the risk of loss, which can be substantial and, depending on the type of product, can exceed the amount of money deposited in establishing the position. Market participants should put at risk only funds that they can afford to lose without affecting their lifestyle.

data.title: IYH: Elevated Valuations And Slowing Growth Signal Subpar 2026 Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH) warrants a hold rating due to concerning signals from its top healthcare holdings, including reduced sales growth, external pressures, and high valuations. These factors will contribute to underperformance for the fund overall compared to similar healthcare funds.

iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF - Overview and Compared ETFs

IYH is an ETF that seeks to capture a broad composition of U.S. holdings within the healthcare sector. With its inception in 2000, the fund has 103 holdings and $3.6B in AUM. IYH is entirely focused on the healthcare sector, with top-weighted industries of pharmaceuticals (33.7%), biotechnology (20.8%), and healthcare equipment (20.6%). I wrote previously on IYH and rated the fund a hold. For the reasons I discuss in this article, I believe the fund still warrants a hold.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are VanEck Biotech ETF (BBH), iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (IHF), and iShares Global Healthcare ETF (IXJ). BBH captures holdings within the healthcare sector that are focused on genetics analysis, pharmaceuticals, and diagnostic equipment. IHF offers investors with a slightly different aspect of the healthcare sector by capturing health insurance, diagnostics, and treatment companies. IXJ differs from IYH in that it seeks to capture global healthcare holdings as opposed to strictly companies within the United States.

Funds Compared: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

IYH has seen the best 10-year average annual return of 9.44%. By comparison, BBH has seen the worst performance with a 10-year average annual return of just 4.54%. IHF and IXJ have 10-year average annual returns of 8.13% and 8.17%, respectively. As a result, each fund has underperformed the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, over the past decade. However, for reasons discussed in this article, I believe IYH will underperform even its own historic average looking forward.

While there are multiple healthcare-focused ETFs with extremely low fees, such as Vanguard’s Healthcare ETF (VHT), the compared funds covered in this article all have similar fees. Additionally, IYH’s dividend yield is comparable with peer healthcare funds.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

IYH

BBH

IHF

IXJ

Expense Ratio

0.38%

0.38%

0.40%

Dividend Growth 5 YR CAGR

6.19%

9.49%

6.93%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 10 Jan 26

IYH Holdings and Key Outlook Factors

Despite each fund focusing on the healthcare sector, different tracked indexes result in very different holdings mixes. This is particularly true for BBH and IHF which track biotech and healthcare providers, respectively. IYH is similar to other broad-based healthcare funds like IXJ which include Eli Lilly (LLY), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and AbbVie (ABBV) as top holdings.

Top 10 Holdings for IYH and Compared Healthcare ETFs

IYH – 103 holdings

BBH – 26 holdings

IHF – 62 holdings

IXJ – 114 holdings

LLY – 14.59%

AMGN – 14.86%

UNH – 22.72%

LLY – 10.60%

JNJ – 8.31%

GILD – 13.81%

CVS – 12.31%

JNJ – 6.10%

ABBV – 6.66%

VRTX – 9.23%

ELV – 10.02%

ABBV – 4.87%

UNH – 5.30%

REGN – 7.83%

HUM – 4.65%

UNH – 3.87%

MRK – 4.69%

IQV – 4.72%

CI – 4.65%

ROG – 3.67%

TMO – 3.84%

NTRA – 4.55%

VEEV – 4.53%

AZN – 3.64%

ABT – 3.67%

ARGX – 4.42%

HCA – 4.40%

MRK – 3.39%

ISRG – 3.47%

ALNY – 4.28%

CNC – 3.41%

NOVN – 3.33%

AMGN – 2.98%

INSM – 4.12%

LH – 3.28%

TMO – 2.81%

GILD – 2.52%

ONC – 3.17%

DGX – 3.08%

ABT – 2.70%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 10 Jan 26

IYH’s high concentration on LLY, JNJ, and ABBV will be an influential factor for the fund looking forward. Unfortunately, each of these top holdings have concerning signals that will likely weigh down IYH overall. These factors are discussed in further detail below.

Eli Lilly: GLP-1 Showing Signs of Cracking

The first concerning factor is the GLP-1 market which will greatly impact IYH’s top holding, Eli Lilly. GLP-1, or glucagon-like peptide-1, is a bodily hormone that helps regulate blood sugar levels. Eli Lilly’s GLP-1 product is tirzepatide, which is more commonly known as Zepbound and Mounjaro, used to treat weight loss and diabetes, respectively. With over 50% of Eli Lilly’s revenue coming from these two products, the company faces significant risk if these products decline in sales. The first factor that may impact sales is CVS Health’s (CVS) recent coverage change. Last year, the pharmacy chain stated that it will no longer cover Zepbound but will cover its competitor, Wegovy, made by Novo Nordisk (NVO). While the weight loss drug market continued to climb late last year, Eli Lilly only saw disappointing sales for Zepbound over the previous quarter.

Looking forward, Zepbound will continue to face external pressures impacting its sales. These include a string of lawsuits by patients who have reported stomach paralysis because of taking the product. Additionally, the agreement between the U.S. federal government, Eli Lilly, and Novo Nordisk to lower costs of GLP-1 medications will further pressure Eli Lilly. These factors are compounded by Eli Lilly’s high valuation. With a forward P/E of 44.9, the pharmaceutical company’s future growth is already captured in its share price. Therefore, LLY will weigh down IYH in 2026 and the holding will see lower growth than has been seen in the past couple of years.

Johnson & Johnson: Low Growth and Dividend Payout will Weigh Down the Holding

IYH’s #2 top-weighted holding is Johnson & Johnson at 8.3% weight. The 140-year-old company is another mediocre top holding due to its low growth. While JNJ’s oncology and cardiovascular pharmaceuticals were primary drivers of sales, other segments such as immunology and infectious diseases saw negative growth this past year.

These recent results are also consistent with its historic revenue growth. Since 2016, the healthcare company has seen only a 3% revenue CAGR. Looking forward, the company’s 2.54% dividend yield and 49% payout ratio will limit significant growth. This is because significant dividend payouts limit the research and development necessary for new products. While JNJ’s forward P/E is more reasonably priced than LLY at 18.9, its valuation remains elevated compared to its recent average. Therefore, JNJ represents the second top holding that will prevent significant capital appreciation for IYH looking forward.

AbbVie: New Products Alone Will Not Substantially Boost Profits

The third factor to consider is AbbVie, IYH’s #3 holding, at 6.7% weight. Humira, which predominantly treats inflammation, was once AbbVie’s flagship product. However, due to patent expiration, competitors, and AbbVie’s focus on other products, product sales for Humira declined roughly 50% at the start of 2025. One forecast previously made was that newer products Skyrizi and Rinvoq, which also treat inflammatory conditions, would more than make up for any loss in Humira sales through the end of the decade. While this forecast has been true so far, AbbVie struggles to see significant profitability with a net income margin of only 4%.

Moving forward, AbbVie does have several products under development. However, each of these products are still under FDA review. Given that only about 14% of pharmaceutical products fully pass FDA review, investors should be cautious in banking on AbbVie’s products under development. Without any new approved products, the company will see muted growth consistent with its YoY revenue growth, which has been less than 10%. Combining this outlook with its current valuation, including a forward P/E 57% higher than its own 5-year average, ABBV represents another unimpressive holding for IYH.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

Despite high returns from LLY, JNJ, and ABBV this past year, the fund has underperformed the broader U.S. market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index. Only BBH has outperformed other compared funds, driven by Amgen (AMGN) and Gilead Sciences (GILD).

As a result of the stretched price of its top holdings, IYH has the most unattractive overall valuation compared to peer funds, as measured by P/E and P/B ratios. Given the outlook factors discussed previously and IYH’s overall valuation, I expect the fund to see subpar returns in 2026.

Valuation Metrics for IYH and Peer Healthcare Funds

IYH

BBH

IHF

IXJ

P/E ratio

33.10

26.30

19.29

31.69

P/B ratio

5.45

4.83

2.34

4.58

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 10 Jan 26

Risks to Investors

IYH is highly concentrated in pharmaceutical companies that rely on profits from selling their treatments. However, a key factor for these companies is the skyrocketing costs of drug development. With the average direct research and development cost of a new drug reaching $369 million, pharmaceutical companies must continuously increase sales prices. This translates to a high per capita spending in the United States, which is even higher than all other developed countries.

Rising costs translate to higher costs for patients, which ultimately puts pressure on insurance companies. These higher costs also drive government regulation, to include actions by the current U.S. presidential administration. As a result, pharmaceutical companies will face regulatory pressure despite the exponentially increasing cost to develop new products. This environment represents a significant risk factor for IYH's top holdings.

Concluding Summary

IYH has historically outperformed many peer healthcare ETFs. However, given the outlook of its top holdings, which represent a significant amount of the fund’s weight, IYH has a weaker overall forecast. Additionally, the fund has a relatively high valuation due to the stretched price of its top-weighted companies.

data.title: APi Group: An Interesting Safe Player
data.description:
Shares of APi Group (APG) have been on a tear in 2025, having seen incredible momentum driven by recognition of past performances, an acceleration of organic growth rates, as well as ambitions for 2028.

This makes me pleased that I have gotten to learn this business. While valuations look reasonable here, it is massive momentum that makes me cautious to get involved here just yet, but also very keen to learn more and continue coverage in 2026 in the hope of more interesting entry points.

About the Business

APi Group is a business service provider of fire, life safety, electronics security, elevator, escalator, and specialty services. The company operates in generally fragmented markets, which are large and growing. Simple execution and M&A efforts make the company target above-average growth, standing at the basis of shareholder value creation. The company owns and operates brands like APi, Viking, Chubb, and Saval, among many others.

By now, the business generates about $7 billion in sales, two-thirds from so-called safety services, with the remaining sales generated from specialty services. This key segment even has an outsized EBITDA contribution, responsible for about three-quarters of EBITDA. The company benefits from predictable cash flow, targeting 60% of revenues seen from inspection, service, and monitoring efforts not too long from here.

During the 2010s, the company has seen sales increase from $1.5 billion to $4.0 billion as a mid-teens compounded annual growth rate is split half-and-half between organic and M&A efforts. After an initial plunge around the time of the pandemic, the business got a major impetus after the Chubb acquisition in 2022, setting the business up to advance sales to about $7 billion by now.

The company targets 2028 revenues at $10 billion, or more, seeing margin expansion as well, with EBITDA margins seen up to 16%. Achieving such targets allows for great potential in the business and shares. This should come on top of a solid track record already, as a mere $7 stock in 2017 has made gradual inroads to levels in the low twenties early in 2025. Following a spectacular run higher, shares now trade at all-time highs around $42 per share.

Valuation Perspective

In February of last year, APi reported somewhat stable and disappointing 2024 results, with reported sales up just over a percent and organic sales down nearly a percent, as revenues just topped $7.0 billion. The core safety service segment had a decent year, with revenues growing in a solid fashion to $5.2 billion, with segment margins exceeding 15% of sales. This is complemented by declining sales at specialty services, whose segment margins were stuck at 11% and change.

Overall EBITDA margins rose less than 140 basis points to 12.7%, with EBITDA reported at $893 million. Adjusted earnings came in at $514 million, equal to $1.84 per share; this number was up 16% on the year before. This number stood in sharp contrast compared to GAAP losses reported, largely due to conversion of preferred Series B stock and some other items.

Net debt was reported at $2.2 billion, a substantial amount, but manageable given the growing EBITDA performance, certainly as the preferred stock was eliminated. With shares trading in the low twenties, based on adjusted earnings, the metrics look quite reasonable, with shares trading at a low-teens earnings multiple.

This certainly was the case, as the company guided for 2025 sales at $7.4 billion, plus or minus $100 million. EBITDA was seen at approximately $995 million, plus or minus $25 million, also aided by a full year of contribution from previous acquisitions.

2025 - On Fire

APi started 2025 with a convincing 7% increase in first-quarter sales, as organic growth accelerated to nearly 2%. Following this momentum, the company hiked the full-year guidance, hiking the sales guidance by $100 million and the EBITDA guidance by another $15 million.

Later in the spring, the company announced its 2028 goals, which included the $10 billion revenue target and 16% EBITDA margin target. Second quarter momentum was strong, certainly given sluggish real estate markets, with reported revenues up 15% and organic growth up more than 8%. This means that the full-year sales guidance was hiked by another quarter of a billion to a midpoint of $7.75 billion, although the hike in the EBITDA guidance was much less pronounced, to a midpoint of $1.025 billion.

Third-quarter sales rose as much as 14%, with organic growth reported near 10%. The sales guidance had been hiked by another $125 million to a midpoint of $7.875 billion, although that EBITDA is only seen at $1.03 billion. Strong momentum is mostly showing up in sales rather than EBITDA, suggesting that pricing/tariffs could be more of a driver than organic volume increases.

Net debt is now reported at $2.2 billion, and with EBITDA surpassing the billion-dollar mark, leverage ratios are seen in the low 2s. A modest pace of deleveraging came in part because of modest buybacks and some smaller M&A deals executed upon (so far) in 2025. With a diluted share count of 429 million shares now trading at $42, the market value of the company stands at $18.0 billion, for just over a $20 billion enterprise valuation.

This values the business at around 2.5 times sales, around 20 times EBITDA. Year-to-date adjusted earnings are up $0.16 to $1.04 per share, as the fourth quarter in this business is strong. Given that earnings might top $2 per share, multiples look rather reasonable on that front, certainly given the strong organic growth rates, driven by execution and culture.

A Bolt-On Deal to End 2025

Towards year-end 2025, APi Group reached an agreement to acquire CertaSite, an inspection-first provider of fire and life safety services in the Midwest region. The deal is set to boost annual sales by $90 million, adding over a percent to the near $8 billion revenue base of APi.

Furthermore, the company guided that both sales and EBITDA guidance for 2025 would surpass the midpoint of the previously issued guidance, and moreover, that net leverage is seen below 2 times.

And Now?

What is clear is that shares of APi Group have seen a significant re-rating over the past year. Trading in the low twenties last year, shares commanded a low-double-digit earnings multiple, as shares nearly doubled in the year following.

This came as flattish organic growth accelerated during the year, in tougher conditions, all strong achievements. This has driven a re-rating during the year, yet with shares nearly doubling, the re-rating seems very substantial and perhaps too much in my view.

Frankly, a 20 times earnings multiple looks reasonable given the organic growth that comes near 10%, yet this comes amidst adjusted earnings as well as very low valuations from the get-go. This makes me a bit cautious here, but what seems to be the case is that the business is a strong performer here. While current multiples look reasonable, I fail to see sufficient reason to chase the shares here, although I am very keen to keep up coverage here to learn more about APi in 2026.

data.title: How Much Longer Can We Avoid Thinking About The Long Term? - Weekly Blog #923
data.description:
First Week 2026

Using the performance of equity mutual funds, it was a great week with average gains of more than +2%. If repeated for each week of the year, it would produce returns of over 100%. Even the value of the US dollar rose a bit during the week.

That was the delusional news! What’s even worse, the average commodity fund invested in gold and other precious metals rose +4.02%. Funds owning stocks of gold and other precious metal mining companies gained +5.32% on average through Thursday.

The latter can suffer mining risks, labor strikes, and raised taxes. Historically, gold has been a hedge against the value of a currency, particularly the US dollar. There is also a small industrial market for gold in the electronics market, which might be in the region of $1,000 an ounce.

How much demand for gold jewelry is really demand for a convenient way to pass on its monetary value? I don’t know. Part of the demand for gold as a use in the crypto world is not known by me. All told, I suspect over half of the value of gold is as a substitute for the US dollar.

What Is The Value Of The US dollar?

Something is worth what someone is willing to pay for it. Currently, it appears to be about $0.99 , up from $0.96 . However, the critical question is its worth in the future. That appears to be what someone is willing to pay for it, delivered today or on a specific date and quantity in the future.

According to a paper prepared by the National Bureau of Economic Research twenty five years ago, people believed the US fiscal budget looking forward 10 years would be $5.9 billion, with all public debt paid off by 2006.

The readers of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act now project a 2054 debt to GDP ratio of 199%, incorporating temporary provisions. Net interest payments would rise to 6.3% from 3.2% today.

(I don’t know how to impact these numbers with the increase in gambling. In the first 11 months of 2025, total sports gambling in New Jersey was $67 billion. The rise of non-securities-backed gambling, particularly among the young, appears to be on the rise.)

Why Should We Care?

Even with the increase in retail securities markets investing, institutional investors set the prices of fixed income securities and many large-cap stocks. Most money invested through 401(k) and similar retirement accounts is invested in mutual funds or SMAs.

Insurance companies, endowments, and other institutional investors may increase their investment in foreign securities, which will impact domestic stock prices. Both domestic and foreign-controlled investors may shift some of their investment focus if the dollar becomes weaker.

Leaders Increasingly Think Globally

Foreign leaders have increasingly thought globally in determining their strategies. Our main adversaries, China, Russia, and North Korea are strategists, whereas the US tends to view the world as tacticians through domestic glasses and the next election time scale. Luckily, many of our domestic commercial leaders are increasingly thinking strategically.

Strategies Going Forward

Going forward, we should recognize that the world is changing at a rapid rate, and we need to change with it. Old rules and strategies will change. We must be careful.

Please share your thoughts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TIC Solutions: Valuation Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for TIC Solutions (TIC) is a hold rating. I acknowledge it has a resilient demand profile, growing exposure to secular data center and infrastructure markets, and a strong technical moat. However, valuation is not cheap, and for that reason, I am not giving a buy rating.

Business Overview

TIC was formed through the merger of Acuren and NV5 Global in August 2025. It provides testing, inspection, engineering, certification, and compliance services for critical infrastructure across North America. Some of the assets it covers include pipelines, utilities, industrial facilities, and data centers. The main objective is to help owners meet ongoing safety, regulatory, and operational requirements through recurring inspection and compliance work.

Strong and visible growth

The best part about TIC's equity story is that most of its revenue is non-discretionary, driven by regulations, insurance requirements, and safety standards. Customers can cut or delay many expenses during a macro downturn, but they cannot skip mandatory inspections, as they would risk fines or even shutdowns. As such, the costs of inspections are little relative to the cost of failure. Flip it around, this means TIC has very strong pricing power.

What makes it even better is that TIC is also serving areas with strong secular growth, such as data centers and power grid infrastructure. For context, TIC's legacy business in the oil and gas sector is volatile because spending in that sector often swings with commodity prices. When oil prices fall, producers and refiners typically cut discretionary capital spending and delay non-urgent projects, which can reduce inspection and engineering work. Data centers and power grid infrastructure are different. Demand is driven by the need for electricity and computing, not oil prices.

In the current era of the AI investment supercycle, large data center operators and utility companies have to build and maintain their assets even in a macro downturn because any power interruption is costly (to themselves and to their underlying customers because it means a shutdown of the data center and, very likely, the operations that depend on it). Demand is evidently strong here, as we can see in Q3 2025, when data center revenue doubled y/y. As growth becomes increasingly driven by these higher-value infrastructure markets, I believe the market will begin to value TIC more like a critical technology infrastructure service provider (should yield a higher multiple).

Moat

Now, for TIC's biggest moat, I believe it is the underlying technical expertise. In TIC's scope of work, in the Inspection and Mitigation segment, there is this thing called "call-out" jobs, which are basically jobs that require teams to respond quickly to assess or fix serious issues. Since this work is specialized and urgent, you would need to deploy a skilled technician on-site to make sure it's fixed. This is not your typical job where the technician is sent to check and fix issues in a nice office setting. For example, TIC has technicians certified to hang from the side of a tall structure to perform ultrasonic testing on a weld (using sound waves to detect hidden cracks). Often in refineries, TIC performs on-stream inspections on critical pipes such as hydrocarbon feed lines or reactor outlet piping, which carry extremely hot, pressurized materials and cannot be easily shut down. Hence, instead of stopping operations and losing millions in production, TIC has to inspect the pipes while the plant remains online (think about how complex and dangerous this is).

This kind of work cannot be done by someone that is non-experienced and cannot be automated. In this sense, entry barriers are high because clients require strict certifications and strong safety records before a vendor can even bid. Once TIC proves it can solve problems safely, customers tend to stick with them rather than switch to a cheaper, untested provider. This in turn gives TIC pricing power.

Synergies

The last thing to talk about is the synergies from the merger. In my view, the most important synergy here is the design-to-maintain feedback loop. This is far more powerful than cost cutting (consolidating back-office functions, optimizing insurance spending, and streamlining fleet management) because it locks in the customer for the entire asset lifecycle (hence, preventing competitors from entering). For example, before the merger, NV5 is hired to commission and certify critical systems, especially cooling. To put things in perspective, this is the part of the job scope where the risk is highest, and customers need to trust NV5 enough to engage them. In the past, NV5 would leave after commissioning. The client would later run a separate bid for a firm to do the inspection, and this opens up room for competition to enter (i.e., the potential for a price war goes up).

After the merger, NV5 includes TIC's maintenance work in the original contract. That means TIC is locked in from day one since it does the inspection work. Overall margins should go up since there is no cost to win the customer, pricing pressure is also lower because maintenance is part of a much larger project (TIC can justify a higher price), and the work is faster because TIC already knows the system. All in all, I believe this synergy will create a strong moat that subscale players are unable to copy.

Valuation

Fundamentally, TIC is great, but valuation-wise, it isn't. Even if we use management growth guidance (midpoint of the 3-5% range) and an adj. EBITDA margin of 16% (for FY26) and assume that TIC trades at 10x forward adj. EBITDA, I don't see any attractive upside. I used 10x, as this is the low end of where peers trade at. Peers include SGS SA, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, and Transcat, which have a growth range of 5-7%, an EBITDA margin >16% on average, and trade at 10x forward adj. EBITDA on average. Hence, when I compare TIC against peers, I'd say the highest multiple it could trade is about 10x.

Conclusion

My view for TIC is a hold rating. I like the fundamental aspects of TIC, where it has a non-discretionary revenue base that provides downside protection and has good exposure to verticals with strong secular growth and technical barriers to entry. However, valuation is not cheap, in my view. Even using management's guidance and a reasonable multiple, I don't see attractive upside.

data.title: SPE: How To Profit From Activism In The Closed End Fund Space
data.description:
The Special Opportunities Fund, Inc. (SPE) is run by Bulldog Investors and specializes in opportunistic investing in closed-end funds, BDCs, and SPACs. Bulldog Investors is a well-known closed-end fund activist.

I usually attend the SPE shareholder meeting every December but was unable to attend this year. I was surprised to find out that only one SPE shareholder attended this year's meeting. It seems like the shareholder base has become less engaged with the fund.

SPE has been a reasonable performer over the last year, and the discount has generally been at the 10% level or below lately. SPE has an accretive share repurchase program in place, but they generally do not use it unless the discount gets up above 12%. Since September, 2024, there has only been one buyback of 5,000 shares of common stock, which was in April 2025. (Fund Shares Outstanding, Repurchases & Conversions)

In a recent Navigator podcast, Phil Goldstein discussed changes to the CEF activist space. Bulldog Investors is still an important CEF activist, but Saba Capital has now become the major player:

"Obviously the biggest change has been the emergence of Saba, which has a lot of capital to be able to employ activist techniques that take very large positions and challenge even major investment managers like BlackRock. The average activist can't emulate that because they've been involved in a significant number of lawsuits and they have the firepower to handle that, and they've been fairly successful at that but that's a big risk that you might have to spend a lot of money, because the funds are spending your money to sue you, or vice versa, to defend themselves. But Saba's dominance of the activist market has pretty much put everybody to the side, we are probably the only other firm that's still activist, and we haven't been that active in closed-end funds."

The Special Opportunities Fund is certainly not your typical equity fund. They use a variety of strategies designed to exploit inefficiencies in the CEF market, SPACs, and other special situations.

For income investors, SPE recently provided updated dividend declarations for the first quarter of 2026 and a substantial year-end "excise tax" distribution that pushed the trailing 12 month yield well into double digits.

On January 2, 2026, SPE announced its monthly distributions for the first quarter of 2026. The fund maintained its steady policy of paying out 8% of the year-end NAV. The actual monthly payment did slip slightly to $0.1087 per share from last year's $0.1098. At current market prices, this represents a forward distribution yield of about 8.73%.

But the total yield is actually somewhat higher. In December 2025, the fund announced a special year-end distribution of $0.70 per share to avoid excise taxes.

The $0.70 payout is paid out as a mix of cash and stock (80% stock / 20% cash), which allows the fund to retain most of their capital while still rewarding shareholders to some extent.

When combined with the regular monthly payouts, the trailing 12-month yield is currently sitting around 13.5%.

The "Double Discount" Strategy

One of the core strategies used by SPE is to offer shareholders a "Double Discount". The fund itself often trades at a 10% discount or higher to its Net Asset Value (NAV).

The underlying SPE portfolio also holds many other CEFs that also trade at 10% discounts or higher. They also own BDCs trading below book value or other discounted value oriented securities. They also do accretive share buybacks of SPE stock when the discount is above 10%.

By purchasing SPE, investors are essentially buying a basket of assets trading roughly at 80 cents on the dollar. The "alpha" comes from management's activism. Unlike passive investors, Bulldog Investors (led by Phillip Goldstein) actively pushes the management of their portfolio holdings to close those discounts through tender offers, liquidations, or conversions to Open-End funds.

Portfolio Composition: Not Just CEFs

While CEFs and BDCs make up the majority of the portfolio, SPE also offers exposure to several other asset classes.

SPE usually invests around 25% of the portfolio in a low-risk SPAC arbitrage strategy where they invest in SPACs before a business combination is formed. The funds allocated to this strategy is generally equal to the leverage they get from the SPE-C 2.75% convertible preferred. One of the reasons the coupon rate on the preferred is so low is because the SPAC portion of the portfolio is enough to cover the preferred stock outstanding.

SPE also invests in special situations related to mergers, tender offers, rights offerings, and other corporate events.

Risk Factors

SPE usually has a beta around 0.75 compared to the S&P 500. But it still has some risks and some risks to keep an eye on.

The baseline expense ratio is on the high side at about 1.86%. This is not totally unreasonable since SPE is an activist-focused fund that requires more legal and research overhead than the average fund.

While SPE has a below average "beta", it is not completely hedged and is still exposed to some downside during severe market corrections.

Investors should also realize that a significant portion of the 13%+ total yield comes from realized capital gains rather than pure income from dividends, so the managers may need to generate some capital gains on underlying investments to justify the payouts, or the NAV could drop.

SPE- Five Year Discount History

Special Opportunities Fund, Inc.

  • Pays Managed Distribution- 8% NAV

  • Total Investment Exposure= $230 Million

  • Total Common Assets= $174 Million

  • Last Monthly Distribution= $0.1087

  • Annual Distribution Rate= 8.73%

  • Baseline Expense ratio= 1.86%

  • Discount to NAV= -9.80%

  • Average Daily Volume= 30,400

  • Average Dollar Volume= $455,000

  • Effective Leverage: 24.35% (from SPE cvt. preferred)

Summary

Many income investors primarily use low-cost index funds or more generic closed-end funds, which may be "closet-indexers" that are similar to a market benchmark. SPE offers a different way to play the CEF market. You are hiring a specialized legal and financial team to hunt for undervalued assets. They are willing to use activism to try to persuade or force CEF managements to take shareholder-friendly actions to help reduce the discounts.

Overall, SPE has performed reasonably well lately, and some of their activist activities have paid off. The fund has also benefited from Saba Capital activism since they sometimes hold similar positions.

Along with SPE, I have also been a long-term owner of the (SPE-C) preferred stock, which has less upside, but in the worst-case scenario can be redeemed for $25.04 on January 21, 2027.

The bid-asked spread for SPE-C is very wide, partially because advisory firm Focus Partners Wealth owns 48% of the preferred float. Focus Partners also owns 13% of the SPE common.

The SPE-C convertible preferred is still a bit "out of the money". But if SPE performs well over the next year, the convertible feature of SPE-C may kick in and SPE-C could trade at a significant premium above $25. It is currently convertible into 1.6552 shares of SPE common. The conversion ratio increases every time SPE pays out a distribution, and recently was bumped up quite a bit because of the $0.70 special distribution.

The NAV is updated weekly on the SPE website (Fund Net Asset Value and Current Conversion Ratio)

SPE offers a pre-announced monthly income stream locked in for the first quarter of 2026. I would consider SPE to be a Strong Buy when it trades at a 12% discount or higher. At the current 10% discount, I would consider SPE to be a Weak Buy or a Solid Hold.

data.title: The Metals Rally Continues
data.description:
Editor's Note: Originally published at tsi-blog.com on January 13, 2026

[This blog post is an excerpt from a recent commentary at www.speculative-investor.com]

The broad rally in the world of metals has continued during the first full week of 2026. During December the upward price momentum in the white metals (silver and the PGMs) became extreme, but now we are seeing dramatic upward price acceleration elsewhere. In particular, the rises in the prices of copper and lithium have become parabolic.

During the first half of this week, the copper price in the US traded above US$6/pound for the first time ever, and the following chart shows that the price of copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME), which is less affected by tariffs than the price in the US, is now above US$13,000/tonne. Prior to the past two months, it had never been above US$11,000/tonne.

For its part, lithium carbonate priced in Chinese Yuan per tonne (CNY/t) has gained about 120% since June 2025 and about 45% over just the past month. Refer to the following chart for the details. Furthermore, since the yuan has strengthened relative to the US$, the performance in US$ terms has been even better.

We wrote numerous times over the past two years that the gold bull market would broaden to encompass almost all commodities, starting with the white metals and then moving to other industrial metals and lastly to oil.

We noted soon after it happened that the tariff-related panic in April 2025 had marked a turning point for the white metals relative to gold, while industrial metals in general have been strengthening relative to gold since October 2025. Therefore, it is fair to say that the broad strength that we are seeing across the metals markets was anticipated.

At this point in the cycle, it’s very unlikely that industrial metals such as copper and lithium are close to major price tops. On the contrary, the rallies from last year’s lows probably are just the initial rallies - the first of two or three bull market legs.

However, like the price action in silver and the PGMs, the price action in the copper and lithium markets is setting the stage for multi-month corrections. It also will be increasing costs in many industries, which is a related issue because there will be demand destruction if prices continue to rise rapidly.

For example, in response to the huge recent price rise in silver, solar cell manufacturers, the largest industrial consumers of silver, are finding ways to substitute base metals for silver in their products.

So, don’t forget to take some profits!

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LGI's Global Mandate Fails To Deliver Growth Or Defense Consistently
data.description:
The Lazard Global Total Return and Income Fund Inc (LGI) has benefited over the past 5 years from an unprecedented bull run in the equity markets. This regime has meant that NAV has held up over a 5-year period, despite a healthy double-digit yield. However, even in this conducive regime, it has lagged SPY (a broader market growth equity equivalent).

The active methodology, which has significantly lagged SPY so far, is unlikely to deliver total return alpha going ahead, particularly in regimes where the equity market growth simply slows down (even if it isn't correct). The drawdown benefits are nothing material to write home about either. And the international mix does not serve its purpose of either boosting returns or diversifying geographical risks. Overall, it is hard to imagine a use case for LGI - hence I consider it a Hold at best.

Portfolio Analysis

The active portfolio (I downloaded it from the fund's website) shows currency hedges for its international exposure. Leaving that aside, the portfolio has around 80 holdings, of which 12 holdings are what appears to be an income sleeve. This sleeve consists of international sovereign bonds, government obligations, domestic bonds, etc., accounting for 10% of the portfolio by weight (calculated using market values reported). The remaining 90% of the portfolio is invested in equities.

The equity portfolio appears to be global mega-cap growth themed. The sectoral allocation shows a reasonable tech tilt upholding growth ambitions but also balances reasonable allocations in financials, industrials, and healthcare that could generate dividends and subdue broad market equity drawdowns.

The top 10 holdings account for ~30% of the portfolio by weight. This is not a heavily concentrated portfolio but remains heavily dependent on equity beta - the 10% income sleeve is not the dominating ballast that its inclusion should introduce.

Half of the top 10 holdings are US mega-cap tech and platform companies. TSMC and ASML add cyclical semiconductor exposure, and some financials are exposed to rate sensitivities. Overall, the portfolio does not appear to aim for defensive ballast, yet it includes some income components and ends up delivering neither concentrated growth that could fund income nor sufficient defensive ballast during drawdowns (as we will see in the Performance Section). This also implies that the fund relies heavily on capital appreciation of the underlying to fund its payouts. In adverse markets, strong ROC components are to be expected in payouts. The underlying is not sufficiently dividend-oriented, nor does it have a material fixed income presence or even option strategies to enhance income and fund the double-digit yields.

I also saw more than half the portfolio invested in the US, with the total weights, including more correlated developed markets, showing up as upward of 75%. There is some emerging market exposure too, but not dominant enough to deliver higher growth or materially alter risk profiles compared to a US-focused portfolio.

Performance Implications

LGI's yield has been consistently seen in double digits across the past decade and across cycles. The fund definitely lives up to its yield and income promise.

However, the income distribution systematically pressures the NAV. We do not see any long-term erosion in LGI's share prices (assumed to be a proxy for NAV) because the yields are funded by the capital appreciation of the underlying. However, the effect of the payouts is still visible on the share prices, which have hardly moved in comparison to not only SPY but also another income vehicle, DIVO (compared for context), that also yields a lower but still healthy ~6% annually.

More importantly, a total return comparison between SPY, DIVO, and LGI confirms the lack of growth alpha that could be relied upon for consistent income. A 3-year rolling total return chart shows periods of decent performance but unreliable average trends. Several times, LGI sees sub-zero 3-year total returns and only seems to do well in patches - like in 2019, 2023 (after the 2022 drawdowns), and 2025. In comparison, DIVO (an income comparison) and SPY (a broad market comparison) appear less volatile while delivering higher average growth - something an income-focused fund like LGI should be expected to do.

The drawdowns are not too reassuring either. During the 2022 downturn, LGI saw sharper drawdowns than even SPY, making it neither a reliable income nor growth vehicle, nor a defensive one.

A Hold for Growth Spurts, Diversification

The 19(a) notices I downloaded for April, October, and November (2025) do not show a trend in terms of what to expect from a tax classification perspective. A couple of insights still emerge beyond the lack of a stable trend in payout distributions.

One, the ROC component is heavier when markets are weaker because the income is not funded by underlying capital appreciation. It increases materially when markets are weak (like in April 2025). Overall, even in strong markets, a ROC of ~20-40% can be expected, and that is still reasonably tax-friendly because it defers taxes. LGI could be relatively attractive for taxable accounts (compared to dividend equity ETFs, REITs, or even covered call strategies).

Except for the slight tax advantage, overall, LGI earns a Hold rating because it does not stand out in comparison to more stable income alternatives (like, say, DIVO). The only case for LGI in your income portfolio is that it delivers spurts of growth. Since the portfolio is active, unless there is a sustained performance trend, income investing should underweight LGI in the income portfolio. If I strictly go by the portfolio composition, the mega-cap loading is reasonable - something that could hold it in good stead for the current regime, where big tech and mega-caps are dominating market leadership.

The other reason why I am okay to keep LGI in an income portfolio is its global diversification. Although past performance hardly shows any meaningful risk absorption during drawdowns (like in 2022), the presence of emerging markets (or ex-developed economies) is still healthy compared to, say, ACWI, where the US exposure is even higher (~63%). The active picks may have undone the geographical diversification in the past, but the risk diversification benefits theoretically stands - although not materially performance altering, as we have noted before.

data.title: Why GPIX Stands Out For Its Capture Ratios Among Similar ETFs
data.description:
Covered call funds are popular for a reason. They monetize volatility for higher yield at the cost of upside cap. But, high yield tells only part of the story. Without sustainable growth of capital base (net asset value or NAV per share), yield can stagnate.

Enter capture ratios. Capture ratios can tell a lot about how covered call funds capture upside/downside relative to their benchmark with no covered call overlay. They are a direct result of a fund’s covered call strategy and can signal the fund’s future returns. If capture ratios are analyzed right, investors can steer clear of ETFs whose distributions and returns are likely to lag behind peers.

Among the funds I compare today, Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) stands out for its most favorable capture ratios. This bodes very well for its long-term performance.

GPIX ETF Explained

GPIX holds stocks in the S&P 500. The fund's target yield is about 8.50%, which is not too far from today's 8%. Depending on market conditions, especially volatility, the current yield can deviate from the 8.50% target. The S&P 500 does not have a high yield at around 1.05%. So, the bulk of GPIX yield comes from selling call options on the S&P 500.

Most covered call funds, especially the older one, use a somewhat inflexible covered call strategy. It does not vary much with market conditions. Conversely, GPIX does vary its portfolio coverage in a systematic way with the target yield in mind. I think Goldman Sachs designed GPIX with the goal of minimizing net asset value, or NAV erosion.

GPIX's focus is to sell at-the-money call options. At-the-money call options come with higher premium income than the out-of-the-money ones. But, they cap upside more when the S&P 500 makes bullish moves.

To counter this negative, Goldman Sachs came up with the "dynamic" portfolio coverage. GPIX does not sell call options to cover a fixed portion of its portfolio. Instead, that coverage varies from 25% to 75%. This percentage is driven by the volatility of the underlying index and how much yield GPIX needs to achieve in a given period.

The main benefit of this approach is that it does not cap GPIX's upside by a lot. This allows the fund to rally with the S&P 500 with very strong upside capture ratios. So, let's define and compare capture ratios of GPIX with other popular ETFs with the S&P 500 as their benchmark.

Capture Ratios Explained

Capture ratios can convey a lot about the risk profile of a covered call fund. The formulas are straightforward:

The upside capture ratio looks at a fund’s returns on up days/months relative to the benchmark. It is the opposite for the downside capture. Ideally, you want a fund with an upside capture as high as possible and downside capture as low as possible.

Investors buy covered call funds with the hope of continuous bull markets. Thus, the worst outcome is when upside capture is below the downside capture. In other words, a fund takes all the beating in bear markets, but is very slow to recover in later rallies.

Let’s consider a hypothetical example. Suppose the S&P 500 goes down by 10% in year 1 and then rallies by 25% in year 2. We have two funds to compare with different capture ratios. Fund 1 and Fund 2 are similar in a sense that their capture ratios are equal. But, fund 2 ratios are lower.

Hypothetical Upside/Downside Capture Example

S&P 500 Fund 1 Fund 2
Base $100.00 $100.00 $100.00
Downside Capture 100% 85% 65%
Upside Capture 100% 85% 65%
Year 1 -10.0% -8.5% -6.5%
Year 2 25.0% 21.3% 16.3%
Balance Year 1 $90.0 $91.5 $93.5
Balance Year 2 $112.5 $110.9 $108.7

We can see that fund 2 is the most defensive one, with a downside capture of only 65%. But when markets rally, fund 2 will be a laggard. Fund 1 is more aggressive and captures a lot of downside. But it will capture a lot of upside too. In bull markets, fund 1 is a clear winner. As it captures more up-moves of its benchmark, fund 1 will go up more.

GPIX vs. SPYI vs. JEPI vs. XYLD

Let’s take a look at four popular covered call ETFs based on the S&P 500. They are:

- Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX)

- JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI)

- NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI)

- Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD)

Note that JEPI does not hold stocks as in the S&P 500 one-for-one. Instead, it selects securities to minimize the volatility of its portfolio. I compiled the upside/downside capture ratios for all funds either from their inception or 10-year time horizon, whichever is available. Here is the data for them with the S&P 500 as their benchmark.

The goal for these funds is to be below the 45% line. In other words, you want to hold a fund whose upside capture is above its downside capture. Anything close to the 45% line is fine, but will appeal to different risk profiles.

GPIX ETF is the most aggressive one with the highest upside/downside capture ratios. This is thanks to that variable portfolio coverage (overwrite levels) I talked about.

Conversely, JEPI takes the most defensive stance. JEPI's goal is to minimize volatility relative to the S&P 500. It won't go down by as much, but it won't go up by as much either. JEPI is an older generation of covered call funds that goes heavy on writing covered calls through so-called equity-linked notes, or ELNs. In up-trending markets, JEPI will lag behind its peers and may be subject to material NAV erosion risk.

Conversely, we have SPYI as a middle-ground covered call ETF. SPYI prioritizes higher yield and is more aggressive when writing call options. But, it takes an active approach. SPYI manages its portfolio in such a way that it has a robust upside capture ratio of ~74%.

Finally, we have XYLD. The fund writes at-the-money call options. It does not use variable portfolio coverage like what GPIX does. This inflexible structure produces an unattractive capture profile. Its upside capture is below its downside capture. All of this produced subpar long-term results. Very often, XYLD struggles with recovery from down episodes and exhibits signs of NAV erosion.

How Capture Ratios Translate Into Total Returns

Let's see total returns and volatility-adjusted metrics for these funds and observe how they relate to upside/downside capture ratios.

Return Metrics for Nov 2023 - Dec 2025

Metric JEPI GPIX SPYI XYLD SPDR S&P 500 ETF
Start Balance $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00
End Balance $12,995.00 $15,845.00 $14,887.00 $13,519.00 $16,776.00
Annualized Return 12.85% 23.67% 20.16% 14.93% 26.97%
Standard Deviation 7.49% 9.77% 8.04% 6.19% 11.52%
Maximum Drawdown -5.67% -6.81% -5.51% -7.01% -7.58%
Sharpe Ratio 1.04 1.77 1.76 1.54 1.75
Upside Capture 53% 86% 73% 52% 100%
Downside Capture 78% 85% 74% 46% 100%
Current Yield 7.40% 8.04% 11.61% 10.45% 1.06%

Source: Portfolio Visualizer

Focusing on yield alone would steer an investor to either XYLD or SPYI. However, high yield alone may produce subpar total returns if looked at in isolation. In this sense, upside/downside capture ratios help gain insights into how different covered call strategies translate into total returns.

Since November 2023, the S&P 500 has almost been on an uninterrupted uptrend. Because of GPIX capturing more upside, it clearly outperformed other funds. SPYI came out as second. It has a very attractive Sharpe Ratio thanks to its lower downside capture. Its heavier use of covered calls lowered its downside and reduced its standard deviation.

JEPI was the biggest laggard due to how it captures the lowest upside from the S&P 500. Surprisingly, XYLD outperformed JEPI. That's despite its less attractive upside vs downside capture in the last 10 years. The culprit for this discrepancy is how these capture ratios may vary from period to period.

As it happened, JEPI had a worse downside capture from November 2023 to December 2025. That explains it. But, I still expect JEPI to beat XYLD on a long-term horizon. JEPI sells slightly out-of-the-money call options. This allows it to take part in future rallies better than XYLD.

Investors Takeaways

Looking at capture ratios can tell a lot about how a fund's covered call strategy translates into its returns. They are largely the results of how a given fund manages its written option positions.

Ideally, an investor would want a high upside and a low downside capture. But, funds like that are rare. Based on today's analysis, most covered call funds have upside and downside capture ratios very close to each other.

SPYI would be best when the uptrend is not very pronounced, with some up and down movements. Because SPYI targets a higher yield compared to its peers, its upside capture is lower than for GPIX. But its active approach is its valuable asset that keeps upside/downside capture in line.

JEPI is probably the third best. It will do well when markets keep falling. Its low downside capture will keep a lid on its drawdowns. But, I don't think this is a sustainable long-term approach. Investors buy covered call ETFs for their long-term appreciation that these funds use to generate yield. With low upside capture, JEPI is very likely to show distributions that either stagnate or grow at low rates compared to SPYI or GPIX.

As for XYLD, its long-term upside capture turned out to be lower than its downside capture. If this relationship persists into the future, XYLD is likely to underperform all the other three funds that I looked at today. Not only will its distributions stagnate or grow slowly, its total returns will be subpar compared to JEPI, SPYI, and GPIX.

Finally, in a long-term uptrend, a higher upside capture should beat a low upside capture, keeping other things constant. We saw how GPIX is doing better thanks to its variable portfolio coverage for covered calls. It manages to trend up well when the S&P 500 goes up. And even though it may go down by more, it will recover much faster and overtake the other funds long-term. GPIX is probably the best-covered fund of the four that I looked at under this scenario.
